figures.gloss: The leader of the Ajivikas — a naked wandering ascetic of the fifth century BCE, said (by his enemies) to have been born in a cowshed and to have walked six years as Mahavira's companion before their bitter rupture. He taught that all beings are carried by niyati, impersonal fate, to an end fixed in advance.
figures.facet: A founder known only through the people he lost arguments with: every biographical detail arrives pre-poisoned by Jain and Buddhist polemic, making Gosala the mirror's test case for reading a tradition through hostile glass.
figures.provenance: Bhagavatī Sūtra 15 (Jain); Sāmaññaphala Sutta, DN 2 (Buddhist); A. L. Basham, History and Doctrines of the Ājīvikas (1951).

figures.gloss: Absolute determinism: there is no cause, human or divine, for the purity or defilement of beings — all are bent by fate, circumstance, and nature (niyati-saṅgati-bhāva), and effort, vow, and deed change nothing of the outcome.
figures.facet: Cosmic order radicalized until agency vanishes — the one shard in the mirror where the impersonal law has neither lawgiver above it nor any lever left for the human hand.
figures.provenance: The Ajivika credo as quoted in the Sāmaññaphala Sutta (DN 2); Basham (1951).

figures.gloss: Every soul must traverse the same fixed course of rebirths — 8,400,000 great aeons in the Buddhist report — and then release happens of itself, 'as a ball of thread, thrown, unwinds to its very end.' Neither virtue shortens the thread nor vice lengthens it.
figures.facet: The only soteriology in the mirror where liberation is a timetable rather than an achievement: everyone is saved, no one is saved early, and the wise and the fool cross the finish line by the same clock.
figures.provenance: Sāmaññaphala Sutta (DN 2) — the doctrine and the thread-simile survive inside the hostile Buddhist account; Basham (1951).

figures.gloss: Ajivika mendicants went naked, begged under strict self-imposed rules, and practiced severe austerities, in some accounts ending life by ritual starvation — a regimen as harsh as anything among their Jain rivals.
figures.facet: The paradox their opponents seized on: why mortify the flesh if nothing avails? The reconstructed Ajivika answer is that austerity is not a means but a symptom — what beings in their final births are fated to do, the badge of a thread nearly unwound.
figures.provenance: Jain and Buddhist descriptions of Ajivika practice collected in Basham (1951) — hostile sources, read against the grain.

figures.gloss: Humanity ranked in six fixed colour-classes from black up through blue, red, yellow, and white to supremely white — with the great Ajivika masters, Gosala among them, placed at the white summit of the scale.
figures.facet: Refinement without effort: the soul does brighten class by class across its aeons, but by fate's schedule alone — transmutation as something that happens TO the soul, never something it does.
figures.provenance: Aṅguttara Nikāya 6.57, where the scheme is attributed to Pūraṇa Kassapa yet ranks the Ajivika masters supreme; Basham (1951) reads it as Ajivika doctrine — attribution contested, carried as such.

figures.gloss: A heterodox teacher contemporary with the Buddha and Mahavira, reported in the Sāmaññaphala Sutta (DN 2) to have taught akiriyāvāda — the doctrine that no action, however violent or generous, produces merit or demerit for the doer. Later tradition, via the six-abhijāti soul-classification attributed to him in AN 6.57, is read by Basham as folded into the Ajivika fold alongside Gosala.
figures.facet: A doctrine of consequence-free action distinct from Gosala's fatalism: where niyati says the outcome is fixed regardless of effort, Kassapa's akiriyāvāda says the deed itself carries no moral weight at all — two routes to the same erasure of merit, from different premises.
figures.provenance: Sāmaññaphala Sutta (DN 2); Aṅguttara Nikāya 6.57; A. L. Basham, 'History and Doctrines of the Ājīvikas' (1951), on the absorption of Kassapa's following into the Ajivika school.

figures.gloss: A set of Mauryan rock-cut caves in Bihar, dedicated by Aśoka and his grandson Daśaratha (c. 260-230 BCE) as donations to Ajivika ascetics, each cave bearing a short donative inscription naming the school by name — the sect's only surviving physical monuments and its sole non-hostile evidence.
figures.facet: Everything else known of the Ajivikas arrives through the mouths of their rivals; these bare, polished chambers and their brief royal dedications are the one place the school appears in its own right, named by a patron rather than accused by an opponent.
figures.provenance: Aśokan and Daśaratha cave inscriptions at the Barabar and Nāgārjunī hills (c. 260-230 BCE); Basham (1951).

figures.gloss: Gosala's own claimed defense, recorded in the Bhagavatī Sūtra 15, that his soul had passed through a sequence of seven successive bodies before his present one — a doctrine of body-transference offered to explain his authority and his survival of past confrontations.
figures.facet: A founder defending his own career with the same logic his school preaches about the cosmos: personal history, like universal history, is not a story of achievement but a fixed sequence run to its end regardless of the man inhabiting it.
figures.provenance: Bhagavatī Sūtra 15 (Jain), the same source the roster already draws on for Gosala's biography; Basham (1951).

edges.note: Intra-tradition bond: fate's law and fate's timetable — niyati fixes the course, saṃsāra-śuddhi is that course run to its automatic end.
Intra-tradition bond: the paradox at the school's heart — brutal austerity practiced under a doctrine that says practice avails nothing.

GOLD SEAM (hostile-source flag): six years' wandering companions, then bitter rivals — the shared śramaṇa apprenticeship and the rupture are attested only in the Jain canon's own account (Bhagavatī Sūtra 15).
GOLD SEAM: the six colour-classes of souls parallel the Jain leśyā soul-tints — Basham suggests common śramaṇa stock, but with only hostile sources the tier stays low.

GOLD SEAM: the wheel of becoming turns on karma — deeds steering rebirth — while niyati denies deeds any purchase at all; the Pali canon rates Makkhali's fatalism the most pernicious of doctrines, so the opposition itself is attested.
GOLD SEAM: sibling śramaṇa arithmetics of vast cosmic time — the Ajivika count of 8,400,000 great aeons survives embedded in the Buddhist report of the rival doctrine.

GOLD SEAM: fellow heterodox teachers among the six of the Sāmaññaphala Sutta — the same Gangetic śramaṇa generation, attested side by side, though doctrinal opposites: annihilation at death against fated aeons of rebirth.

Kassapa's akiriyāvāda (no act bears moral fruit) and Gosala's niyati (all outcomes are pre-fixed) are sibling erasures of moral consequence, arriving by different arguments — action-negation versus outcome-determinism.

AN 6.57 attributes the six-fold soul classification to Kassapa himself, even as it ranks the Ajivika masters supreme within it — attribution contested, carried as such by the roster's own six-abhijatis entry.
Contemporary heterodox teachers whose followings a later tradition (Basham) reads as merging into one Ajivika fold.
Fellow members of the six heterodox teachers named together in the Sāmaññaphala Sutta (DN 2) — the same Gangetic śramaṇa generation, attested side by side.
The caves' Mauryan donative inscriptions are the school's sole physical monument, naming the Ajivikas by name as recipients of Aśoka's and Daśaratha's grants.
Gosala's own claimed defense of his career and authority, recorded in the same Bhagavatī Sūtra 15 source as his biography.
The seven-body sequence recasts personal history as a fixed run to its end, the same logic niyati applies to the cosmos at large.
fellow figures of the same Gangetic śramaṇa debate-generation, attested in the same canonical dialogues, though doctrinal opposites — Pāyāsi's empirical annihilationism against Ājīvika fatalism.

dossier.essence: An extinct order of naked wandering ascetics in ancient India, led by Makkhali Gosala, a fifth-century BCE contemporary and rival of Mahavira and the Buddha. Its central claim was niyati — absolute, impersonal fate: no deed, vow, or effort alters the destiny of any being. Once a major śramaṇa movement patronized by Mauryan emperors, it survives only in the hostile reports of its Jain and Buddhist opponents, in a handful of inscriptions, and in Tamil sources.
dossier.cosmos: All beings are driven through a fixed course of transmigration by niyati; there is no cause, human or divine, for their purity or defilement, and no lever of karma or grace. The Buddhist report assigns the course 8,400,000 great aeons, after which release happens of itself, 'as a ball of thread, thrown, unwinds to its very end' — everyone is liberated, no one early. Souls were ranked in six fixed colour-classes (abhijatis) from black to supremely white, brightening across the aeons on fate's schedule alone.
dossier.practice: Ajivika mendicants went naked, begged under strict self-imposed rules, and practiced austerities as severe as their Jain rivals', in some accounts ending life by ritual starvation — conduct their opponents mocked as pointless under a doctrine that denies effort any effect, and which Basham reconstructs as the fated badge of a soul in its final births. The order was substantial enough to receive imperial patronage: Aśoka and his successor Dasharatha dedicated the Barabar and Nagarjuni cave-dwellings to the Ajivikas in the third century BCE, and Tamil inscriptions trace communities into the thirteenth-fourteenth century CE.
dossier.canon: No Ajivika text survives; the doctrine is preserved in quotation inside opponents' scriptures — chiefly the Buddhist Sāmaññaphala Sutta (Dīgha Nikāya 2) and the Jain Bhagavatī Sūtra 15 — and reconstructed from these and Tamil sources by Basham.
dossier.reflects: Fatalism carried to completion — cosmic order absolutized until human agency vanishes, yet still generating discipline, community, and patronage; and the historiographic condition of being knowable only through enemies.
sources: A. L. Basham, 'History and Doctrines of the Ājīvikas: A Vanished Indian Religion' (1951)

links.cite: A. L. Basham, 'History and Doctrines of the Ājīvikas: A Vanished Indian Religion' (1951)
